title: Creating a draught lobby with a glazed porch
subtitle: Adding a porch enclosure to prevent the front door opening directly into a heated hall
excerpt: A front door that opens directly into a hallway loses a significant volume of heated air every time it opens. A glazed porch creates a draught lobby: a small intermediate space that breaks the air path between inside and outside. This is the decision guide and basic specification for a simple lean-to timber or uPVC porch enclosure.
sourceNotes: CIBSE Guide A (2015) on infiltration and ventilation. Building Regulations Approved Document B (fire safety) on lobby requirements. gov.uk planning portal guidance on permitted development for porches. Energy Saving Trust guidance on reducing infiltration heat loss.

glossaryTerms.definition: An enclosed entry zone between an exterior door and the interior of a building, designed so that the exterior door closes before the interior door opens. Prevents direct air exchange between outside and the heated interior on each entry.

Heat lost through uncontrolled air leakage and air exchange, as opposed to conductive heat loss through walls and windows. Repeated door opening is a form of short-duration infiltration that can account for 5 to 15% of total heat loss in an exposed location.

content.text: Every time a front door opens, a column of warm air at floor-to-ceiling height is exchanged with cold outside air. In a terraced house on an exposed road, this loss happens eight to fifteen times a day in winter. A
breaks this: the outer door is closed before the inner door opens. The reduction in

through door opening is 60 to 80% for an average household. The wider thermal benefit of the porch enclosure itself, which reduces wind exposure on the front wall and the front door, adds further saving.

A porch in England is permitted development if it is under 3 m² in floor area, no part exceeds 3 m above ground level, and the house is not in a conservation area, AONB, or National Park. Check with your local planning authority before ordering. Rules differ in Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland.
Minimum specification for a thermal benefit

The porch needs to be large enough that the outer door is fully closed before the inner door is opened. The minimum depth from outer door to inner door is 900 mm; 1,200 mm is comfortable. The outer door and the porch glazing should have weather-seals and a low threshold to prevent wind bypass. An unglazed lean-to or open canopy does not create a draught lobby effect: it must be enclosed on three sides and overhead.

Timber-framed with double-glazed units is the most thermally effective option and can match the house character. uPVC is lower-maintenance and commonly available from window companies who also replace windows. Polycarbonate lean-tos are the cheapest option (600 to 1,200 pounds installed) but the twin-wall polycarbonate roof panels typically have a U-value of around 1.5 to 2.5 W/m²K, which limits the overall porch thermal performance. Glass-roofed porches perform better but cost more.

A 1930s semi in a suburban road: 12 door-openings per day in winter. Each opening exchanges approximately 1 m³ of 20°C air for 5°C air. Energy loss per opening: roughly 6 Wh. Total per day: 72 Wh, or around 26 kWh over a 6-month heating season. That gives annual saving of around 1.5 pounds at 2025/26 cap rates on the door-opening loss alone. The wider porch thermal benefit (reduced wind speed on the front wall) is harder to quantify but likely contributes a similar amount. Payback on a 2,500-pound porch from thermal savings alone is long; most buyers value the space and covered entry independently.

An unventilated porch accumulates condensation on the glazing in cold weather. Include a trickle vent in the side glazing or a 5 to 8 mm gap under the outer door threshold to prevent condensation damage.
